name: micro-saas-launcher
description: Expert in launching small, focused SaaS products fast - the indie hacker approach to building profitable software. Covers idea validation, MVP development, pricing, launch strategies, and growing to sustainable revenue. Ship in weeks, not months. Use when: micro saas, indie hacker, small saas, side project, saas mvp.
capabilities: Validate SaaS ideas using structured frameworks before building
Define MVP scope with 2-week development timelines
Design pricing strategies for micro-SaaS products
Create launch playbooks for early customer acquisition
Identify and avoid common micro-SaaS anti-patterns
author: sickn33
securitySummary: This skill contains documentation-only content with no executable code. All 21 static analysis findings are false positives - the detected patterns appear within markdown code blocks that serve as illustrative examples, not actual runnable code. No scripts, network calls, filesystem operations, or command execution present.
valueStatement: Building a SaaS product often takes too long and fails to validate market demand. This skill provides proven frameworks to validate ideas, ship MVPs quickly, and grow profitable micro-SaaS businesses using indie hacker strategies.
limitations: Does not execute code or deploy applications
Does not directly integrate with external services like Stripe or Supabase
Provides guidance and frameworks, not turnkey solutions
Does not replace legal or financial professional advice

useCases.title: First-Time SaaS Founder
useCases.description: A developer with a product idea needs structured guidance on validating demand and building an MVP quickly without wasting months on features users do not want.

useCases.targetUser: Side project builder transitioning to entrepreneurship
useCases.title: Side Project to Business
useCases.description: An indie hacker with a side project wants to transition to a profitable micro-SaaS by implementing proper pricing, launch strategies, and growth tactics.

useCases.title: Rapid Market Testing
useCases.description: An experienced founder wants to quickly test multiple SaaS ideas using lean validation methods to find the most promising opportunity before committing resources.

promptTemplates.title: Validate My SaaS Idea
promptTemplates.prompt: I have an idea for [describe your SaaS concept]. Help me validate this idea using the validation framework. Guide me through answering: does the problem exist, will people pay, can I build it, and can I reach customers?
promptTemplates.scenario: Early stage idea validation before writing any code

promptTemplates.title: Plan My 2-Week MVP
promptTemplates.prompt: My SaaS does [core function]. Help me scope a 2-week MVP using the recommended stack. Break down what to build each day, what features to include, and what to skip for later iterations.
promptTemplates.scenario: Ready to build after validation, need structured development plan

promptTemplates.title: Design My Pricing Strategy
promptTemplates.prompt: My micro-SaaS targets [customer type] and solves [specific problem]. The alternative costs them [time/money]. Recommend pricing tiers and price points using the micro-SaaS pricing framework.
promptTemplates.scenario: MVP is ready, need to set prices before launch

promptTemplates.title: Create My Launch Playbook
promptTemplates.prompt: I am launching my micro-SaaS in [industry/niche]. My MVP does [core feature]. Create a launch playbook covering: pre-launch activities, launch day tasks, and post-launch growth tactics for the first 30 days.
promptTemplates.scenario: MVP complete, preparing for public launch and customer acquisition

bestPractices: Validate with money before building - pre-sell or find paying beta users
Ship MVP in 2 weeks maximum - extend timeline only for critical bugs
Start with higher pricing - discount later rather than raise prices
Build distribution before product - know how you will reach customers
Focus on one core feature - resist adding features until users request them
antiPatterns: Building in secret without user feedback leads to wasted development on unwanted features
Adding features before shipping the core functionality delays launch and revenue
Pricing too low attracts price-sensitive customers and makes business sustainability difficult
Choosing complex technology stacks increases development time and maintenance burden

faq.question: How long should I spend validating before building?
faq.answer: Spend 1-2 weeks on validation maximum. Talk to 5-10 potential users, attempt pre-sales, and research competitors. If you cannot find evidence of demand in 2 weeks, pivot to a different idea rather than continuing to validate indefinitely.

faq.question: What is a realistic revenue goal for a micro-SaaS?
faq.answer: A successful micro-SaaS typically generates $1,000-$10,000 MRR within the first year. Focus on profitability over growth - a solo founder can live well on $5,000 MRR with minimal expenses. Aim for 10-20 paying customers at $50-100/month rather than thousands of free users.

faq.question: Should I offer a free tier or free trial?
faq.answer: For micro-SaaS, a 7-14 day free trial converts better than a free tier. Free tiers attract non-paying users and increase support burden. A trial creates urgency and lets users experience full value. Exception: Offer free tier if your product benefits from network effects or viral growth.

faq.question: How do I choose the right technology stack?
faq.answer: Use established, boring technology you already know. For most solo founders: Next.js for full-stack, Supabase for database and auth, Stripe for payments, Vercel for hosting. Avoid learning new frameworks during MVP development. Your stack choice matters less than shipping quickly.

faq.question: What if a competitor already exists in my space?
faq.answer: Competition validates market demand. Most successful micro-SaaS products have competitors. Your advantage: faster iteration, better customer service, niche focus, or superior user experience. Study competitor reviews to find complaints you can solve. Do not compete on price - compete on specificity and service.

faq.question: How much should I charge for my micro-SaaS?
faq.answer: Price at 20-50% of the alternative cost. If your tool saves 5 hours monthly at $50/hour, charge $50-125/month. Typical ranges: simple tools $9-29/month, pro tools $29-99/month, B2B tools $49-299/month. Start higher than comfortable - you can discount for early adopters but raising prices later is difficult.
